glossaryTerms.definition: A small relief decoration made by pressing clay into a mould, releasing it, and applying it to a pot or slab with slip. The raised surface stands proud of the surrounding clay.
A shallow one-sided mould, traditionally made from bisque clay or plaster, with a recessed relief design. Soft clay pressed into the mould and released emerges as a raised sprig.
The standard method for joining two pieces of clay: scratch the surfaces to be joined, apply liquid clay (slip) to both, then press firmly together.

The stage of drying when clay is firm but still slightly damp - it can be handled without distorting and carved cleanly. For air-dry clay this is typically 1-3 hours after working, depending on humidity.
Any decorative element that projects above the surface of the surrounding clay body, creating a three-dimensional texture or image.

content.text: A
sprig
is a small piece of raised
relief decoration
that you press from a mould and then apply to a clay surface with
score and slip

. The technique has been used in European ceramics for centuries - the Wedgwood jasperware cameo is a famous example. This project scales it down to a no-kiln air-dry version using a found object as the
sprig mould

. A flat button with an interesting surface, a small shell, or a coin all work. The mould is made from a thin bed of air-dry clay pressed around the object, and then used repeatedly. The finished sprig sits on a slab tile as a decorative panel.

materials.name: Found object for mould (button, shell, or coin)
Petroleum jelly or vegetable oil (mould release)
Slip (water mixed with air-dry clay scraps to a cream consistency)
PVA glue (for sealing the finished tile)

content.text: Preparation

Choose a found object with clear surface detail and at least 2 mm of relief depth. Objects that are too shallow, like a thin coin with worn lettering, will produce a sprig that is barely visible. A large shank button, a small decorative shell, or a textured metal token all work well. The object must be non-porous or coated with a release agent so the clay does not stick.

Make slip in advance: mix a small pinch of air-dry clay with water in a jar and stir to a thick cream. This is the adhesive for attaching the sprig to the tile. Fresh slip works better than dried and reconstituted.
Steps

Make the mould. Roll a small piece of clay into a thick disc about 10 mm deep and 5-6 cm wider than the found object on each side. Coat the found object with a thin smear of petroleum jelly - this is the mould release. Press the object firmly face-down into the clay disc, pushing it to roughly half its depth.
Ease the object out carefully. Pull it straight upward without rocking. You should see a clean negative impression in the clay. Let the mould dry to
leather hard
(1-2 hours) before using it. A fully dry mould works even better - it absorbs a little moisture from the pressed sprig clay and helps the sprig release cleanly.

Coat the mould cavity with a light smear of petroleum jelly. Take a small ball of fresh clay (about the size of a marble) and press it firmly into the mould cavity with your thumb. Work from the centre outward so the clay fills the fine detail first.
Trim the sprig flat. Use the fettling knife to slice across the back face of the mould, removing the excess clay in one clean stroke. The back of the sprig should be level with the top of the mould.

Release the sprig. Lay a small square of damp cloth over the mould, press lightly, and lift - the cloth usually brings the sprig with it. Alternatively, tip the mould upside down and tap gently. Place the sprig face-up on a flat surface. If it distorts, re-press it gently and re-trim.
Make the base tile. Roll a slab of air-dry clay to 6 mm thickness and cut a tile to your chosen size, 8 x 10 cm works well. Let it sit for 20 minutes to firm up slightly before attaching the sprig.

Attach the sprig using score and slip. Use the scoring tool to scratch both the back of the sprig and the area of the tile where it will sit. Apply slip to both scratched surfaces using the liner brush, then press the sprig firmly into place. Hold for 10 seconds and check the edges are sealed all around.

Blend the edge join. Use the needle tool or a small piece of damp sponge to blend the very edge of the sprig into the tile surface. A clean, uninterrupted join prevents the sprig from lifting during drying.
Drying and finishing

Dry the tile flat on a sheet of baking paper or a wooden board for 24-48 hours. Cover loosely with a thin cloth for the first few hours to slow the drying and reduce cracking risk. Once bone-dry, sand any rough edges with fine sandpaper. Seal the tile with two thin coats of undiluted PVA, allowing each coat to dry fully. The PVA hardens the surface and gives a light sheen. Paint with acrylics before or after the PVA coat depending on the finish wanted.
Variations
Use multiple sprigs from the same mould in a repeating pattern across a larger tile.
Apply the sprig to a pinch pot or coil-built pot instead of a flat tile, on the outer wall at leather-hard stage.
Mix a small amount of acrylic paint into the clay before pressing to give the sprig a contrasting colour to the base tile.
Make a multi-object mould by pressing several small objects (a row of buttons, a group of shells) into the same clay slab.

items.problem: Sprig tore coming out of the mould
items.cause: Mould was too wet and clay stuck; or not enough mould release
items.fix: Let the mould dry for longer before use, and apply a light coat of petroleum jelly to the cavity before pressing.

items.problem: Sprig detail is blurry - impression is unclear
items.cause: Clay was too soft when pressed in, or the found object has insufficient relief depth
items.fix: Use slightly firmer clay and press harder; choose an object with at least 2 mm of surface relief.

items.problem: Sprig lifted off the tile during drying
items.cause: Score-and-slip join was incomplete or the sprig edge was not blended in
items.fix: Re-wet the join line with a dab of slip and press firmly. Blend the edge with a damp sponge before leaving to dry.

items.problem: Tile cracked across the centre during drying
items.cause: Tile dried too fast, probably from underneath on an absorbent surface
items.fix: Dry on baking paper or a slightly damp cloth to slow moisture loss from the base, and cover loosely for the first 6 hours.
